main_summary.summary: Barbara Edna Vickers (Mrs. Adell Vickers) was interviewed by Joe Mosnier on September 13, 2011, at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine, Florida, for the Civil Rights History Project. Vickers recounts her upbringing in St. Augustine, where she attended St. Benedict the Moor Catholic School and Excelsior High School, graduating in 1942. After leaving St. Augustine to escape its racial caste system, she worked as a tack welder in shipyards during World War II in New York and Seattle (briefly at Boeing Aircraft as a mechanic helper). She returned to St. Augustine after marrying Adell Vickers and opened a beauty salon. In the early 1960s she joined the movement led by her across-the-street neighbor Dr. Robert B. Hayling, participating in kneel-ins at Grace United Methodist Church, traveling to Tallahassee to petition the governor, and serving on the Scott Street network that monitored Klan night-rides and protected Hayling's home. She describes the September 1963 Klan shooting attack on the Hayling residence in which his dogs Madonna and a boxer were killed. The interview's second half documents her decade-long campaign as president of the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Remembrance Committee to build the Foot Soldiers Monument on the central plaza, raising roughly $70,000 with major support from Phil McDaniel, attorney Patrick Canan, Hank Thomas, and a donor she names as Nina Verilam.
key_themes: Dr. Robert B. Hayling and the Scott Street network
Kneel-ins, sit-ins, and swim-ins as direct-action tactics
Klan night-riding and the 1963 attack on Hayling's home
Black women's labor in WWII shipyards
Foot Soldiers Monument and civil rights commemoration
Interracial coalition-building in the monument campaign
main_summary.historical_significance:
Vickers's testimony is a first-person account of the St. Augustine campaign of 1963-1964, one of the SCLC's most violent and least-publicly-memorialized confrontations, and the immediate prelude to the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Her recollection of being recruited by Dr. King in person to participate in church kneel-ins, of the Scott Street mutual-defense network that guarded Hayling's home, and of the September 1963 Klan shooting that killed Hayling's dogs adds grassroots detail to a campaign more commonly narrated through King, Andrew Young, and Ralph Abernathy.

The second half of the interview documents the founding of the Foot Soldiers Monument (dedicated 2011) on St. Augustine's central plaza, the first civil rights monument in a city that, by Vickers's account and Mosnier's framing, had spent decades erasing its movement history from public view. Her decade-long fundraising effort, the fact that most major donations came from white attorneys and businesspeople rather than the local Black church community, and her successful insistence on commemorating the rank-and-file foot soldiers rather than King alone constitute a case study in civil rights memorialization politics.

chapters.title: Interview Opening and Setting
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the interview at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 13, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project, jointly produced by the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. Dr. Robert B. Hayling is present as an observer and will be interviewed separately in the series.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series, 2011

chapters.title: Childhood and Family in St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Vickers describes growing up in St. Augustine, attending St. Benedict the Moor Catholic School through eighth grade and graduating from Excelsior High School in 1942. Her mother worked as a waitress at the Monson Motor Lodge; her grandmother and aunt also worked there. Her father was the head waiter (matre d') at the Bennett Hotel a few doors down. She had two younger sisters, Gwendolyn and Charlotte.
keywords: St. Benedict the Moor Catholic School

chapters.title: Leaving the Jim Crow South
chapters.summary: Vickers describes her determination to leave St. Augustine after high school graduation in 1942, citing daily indignities such as having to wait at the back of grocery store lines when white customers entered and being forced off sidewalks. She moved to New York and worked in a shipyard during the war, learning to tack weld at company-sponsored school. She then relocated to Seattle to work at Boeing Aircraft, where she was reassigned as a mechanic helper rather than a welder.

chapters.title: Return to St. Augustine and Marriage
chapters.summary: Vickers describes returning to St. Augustine in the late 1950s after marrying Adell Vickers, who was a bellhop in New Jersey but refused to settle anywhere except his hometown. She opened a home beauty salon. Adell Vickers worked at a drive-in movie theater, supplemented by gambling, and later became recreation director at the center where Solomon Calhoun (Vickers's high school basketball coach, later city commissioner) had connected him.
related_events: Post-WWII return migration to the South

chapters.title: Meeting Dr. Hayling and Dr. King
chapters.summary: Vickers describes Dr. Robert B. Hayling as a neighbor who lived across the street and led the local civil rights effort. She attended rallies and meetings, where Hayling asked for volunteers for direct-action tactics. The night Dr. King visited a St. Augustine church, Hayling asked for people to integrate white churches, and King personally pointed to Vickers and asked if she would go. She agreed, then immediately wondered what she had committed herself to.

chapters.title: Scott Street Network and Klan Threats
chapters.summary: Vickers describes the Scott Street network of eleven houses where movement participants lived. Julia Conway organized residents to protect Hayling, with the West Side Boys hiding in trees around his house. Vickers details the network's protocol for Klan night-rides: phone alerts, instructions to crawl below window level because the Klan was shooting low, and the practice of crawling between refrigerator and other appliances for cover. She recounts being pulled over by a police officer who tried to charge her tire iron as a weapon.
related_events: 1963 Klan attacks on St. Augustine movement

chapters.title: Shooting at Hayling's Home
chapters.summary: Vickers describes the September 1963 Klan shooting attack on Dr. Hayling's home. The protective network of men guarding the house had fallen asleep. When Vickers arrived home that night, an officer was blocking Scott Street; he allowed her through after she explained she lived there. Julia Conway reported having given the heavyset officer a chair. Hayling's dogs Madonna and a boxer were killed; his wife, pregnant with their third child, narrowly escaped after just having gotten up from a sofa struck by bullets. Hayling sent his children to Tallahassee for safety.
related_events: September 18, 1963 Klan attack on Hayling residence

chapters.title: Petitioning the Governor and Daily Movement Work
chapters.summary: Vickers describes traveling to Tallahassee with Hayling and others, representing the Catholic community, to petition the governor. She recounts the fear of Klan cars running them off the road, as had happened on trips to Butler's Beach. She describes managing her beauty salon during the movement years (covering windows with blankets, losing customers afraid to be seen patronizing a movement participant) and her family's reactions: her grandmother begged her to stop, predicting she would be killed; her sisters declined to participate, one being away at Bethune-Cookman College in Daytona.
related_events: St. Augustine movement appeals to state government

chapters.title: Kneel-Ins at White Churches
chapters.summary: Vickers describes attempting to integrate Grace United Methodist Church, where a white Metropolitan Life insurance agent who collected her premium each Monday stood in front of her kneeling form with his foot positioned as if to kick. She confronted him the next day at her door and dropped the policy. She frames the kneel-ins as part of a broader demand for full citizenship rights, not specifically for the right to eat with white people, rejecting her grandmother's resignation to the Jim Crow status quo.

chapters.title: Encounters with Dr. King and Mrs. Hayling
chapters.summary: Vickers recounts meeting Dr. King at First Baptist Church (after arriving late to St. Paul AME Church) and shaking his hand, recalling his cotton-soft hands and reputation for remembering names. She missed an earlier meeting with King because she was serving as president of the beauticians' association at a Daytona convention, sending Queenie Mitchell McCall in her stead. She describes Mrs. Hayling as quiet and reserved, and recounts a later encounter in Fort Lauderdale where Mrs. Hayling, traumatized, kept crossing the street to avoid contact until Vickers identified herself.

chapters.title: Founding the Foot Soldiers Monument Campaign
chapters.summary: Vickers describes conceiving the Foot Soldiers Monument roughly a decade before the interview. Working through the Council on Aging under director Kathy Brown's Mayor's Senior Advisory Committee, she became president of that committee after the prior president resigned. Brown initially urged her to pursue a Martin Luther King monument for easier fundraising, but Vickers insisted on commemorating the rank-and-file foot soldiers. The committee secured 501(c) nonprofit status and began fundraising.

chapters.title: Fundraising the Monument
chapters.summary: Vickers describes the fundraising sequence: a donor named Nina Verilam contributed $20,000 to retain the sculptor; her grandson secured a UPS grant of approximately $7,400; Phil McDaniel arranged a $100-per-head dinner at attorney Patrick Canan's office that raised $24,000, featuring Vickers, musician Del Kahn, and foot soldier Freddie Thompson (beaten alongside Andrew Young in 1964). Hank Thomas of the Freedom Rides also gave a sizable donation. The total budget reached approximately $70,000. Vickers expresses disappointment that only one Black church in St. Augustine donated.

chapters.title: City Government and Monument Installation
chapters.summary: Vickers describes navigating the City Commission process, including departing from a feuding rival monument group to form her own committee. African American City Commissioner Earl Jones earmarked $5,000 of city money for prep work, and the city later paid for the surrounding walkway with foot-prints. She praises the mayor, city manager, and city attorney for legal cooperation. At the installation ceremony she was overcome and could not speak; Hayling waved her forward and the crowd applauded. Hayling subsequently credited her with the organizational drive that made the monument possible.

chapters.title: Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: Vickers reflects on how the monument campaign changed her perspective on white people by introducing her to committed white allies. She invokes her grandmother's stories of Klan home invasions and lynchings as the historical context against which she measures her own movement participation. At 88 she affirms she is still ready to keep working on civil rights, motivated by reaching young people who can understand what the foot soldiers achieved.

loc_video.caption: Barbara Edna Vickers oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 13
